headline: ACREAGE LANDED; CORN FINDS $4.14 AND WAITS
subheadline: Stocks surprise beat softened the acreage blow, but December corn at $4.38 is still pricing acres, not heat.
lead: The USDA Acreage report landed yesterday, and today corn is telling you exactly what it thinks: $4.14 nearby, $4.38 December, and almost no movement in either direction. The quarterly stocks number came in 14% above last year, which should have been bearish on its own, but Compeer Financial's Megan Roberts flagged what actually happened: stocks came in below trade expectations, and the below-expectations read flipped the sentiment on its head. So corn is not in freefall. It is also not rallying. It is sitting on a data verdict while 90-to-95 degree heat blankets the Corn Belt during the most critical week of pollination, and the December contract is not pricing any of that yet.
the_takeaway: Corn has the data verdict; the heat premium still hasn't shown up in December.
teaser: Acreage is priced. Heat is not. December corn at $4.38 is the number that has to move.

one_number.unit: corn stocks above year-ago, per USDA quarterly report
one_number.context: A 14% year-over-year jump in corn stocks would normally hit futures hard. It didn't, because the trade was positioned for an even larger number and the stocks figure came in below expectations. That spread between bearish math and bullish surprise is the reason corn is at $4.14 and not $3.95 today. The lesson: in grain markets, the absolute number matters less than where it landed versus what the funds were betting.

yesterdays_call.summary: The call was corn higher, targeting a move toward $4.25 on December as the Acreage report absorbed and heat premium began building into the front end.
yesterdays_call.note: Corn did not get there. Nearby settled at $4.14 and December at $4.38, both short of the $4.25 nearby target. The stocks surprise kept corn from breaking lower, but it did not deliver the upside push the call needed. Miss. The heat premium has not been added yet, and $4.25 remains unfinished business if Monday's Crop Progress shows condition deterioration.

sections.title: Corn: Heat Meets the Data Verdict
sections.body: Corn nearby settled at **$4.14**, off less than a quarter-cent. December lost 1.5 cents to **$4.38**. Yesterday's Acreage report was bearish on acres, but the quarterly stocks read saved the session: stocks came in 14% above a year ago in absolute terms, yet below where the trade was positioned, and that below-expectations print flipped the tone. Now the market has its data verdict and is staring directly at a Corn Belt heat wave, with highs of 90 to 95 degrees forecast through the week in most areas east of the southern High Plains. That is peak pollination weather. Heat stress during pollination is the one thing that can reprice the December contract without a single USDA report, and $4.38 is not pricing any stress premium right now. The 6-to-10 day outlook keeps hotter-than-normal conditions in place nationally. If the heat holds and the upper Midwest rain coverage thins, December has room to move toward $4.50 without a single new bushel of news.
sections.catalyst: USDA quarterly stocks below trade expectations offset bearish acreage; Corn Belt heat wave enters pollination window.
sections.bottom_line: Data verdict absorbed; next move belongs to the thermometer, not the calendar.
sections.vs_yesterday: Corn: held well above $3.95 target; stocks surprise changed the post-acreage math.

sections.title: Soybeans: November Slips, Nearby Holds
sections.body: Nearby soybeans barely moved, settling at **$11.14**, up half a cent. November gave back 5.5 cents to **$11.44**. The split matters: old-crop is essentially unchanged while new-crop is softening, and that is carry behavior, not a rally. Soybean meal dropped 0.7% to **$303.20** and soybean oil fell 1.1% to **$64.83**, continuing the pullback from last week's soyoil run. The same Corn Belt heat pattern that threatens corn pollination also accelerates soybean pod-fill timing, but soybeans are less vulnerable at this calendar point than corn. China's $17 billion annual US ag purchase commitment through 2028, announced May 18, has yet to translate into a sustained futures bid past the initial bounce, and the November contract at $11.44 is drifting rather than building. No clean catalyst for today's new-crop softness. Watch Thursday's export sales: below 300,000 metric tons keeps November under pressure.
sections.catalyst: Soyoil/meal complex continues post-run pullback; China purchase commitment not yet showing in new-crop bids.
sections.bottom_line: Old-crop steady, new-crop drifting; Thursday export sales are the next real read.
sections.farmer_action: Producers with unpriced November beans: $11.44 is carry, not a signal. Hold and watch Thursday's export number before deciding.

sections.title: Cattle: Lockout Clock Keeps Ticking
sections.body: Live cattle settled at **$242.25**, off 0.5%. Feeders gave back 0.8% to **$364.35**. The ongoing Cargill Fort Morgan/Schuyler plant lockout that began Tuesday May 19 is still the load-bearing story here: 1,700 workers out, roughly 6,000 head per day removed from processing, and no resolution announced. The box beef cutout has not produced a fresh catalyst to break the pattern either way. Live cattle have now been grinding in the low $240s since the lockout created processing-constrained dynamics three weeks ago, and the chart damage done in May has not been repaired. Feeders at $364.35 remain near their session range ceiling but have not reclaimed the levels seen before the lockout began. The feeder-to-live ratio at current prices sits near 1.50, historically wide, and that spread keeps telling you the market is not done repricing the processing constraint. No resolution signal from Cargill means wait is the right verb.
sections.catalyst: Cargill Fort Morgan/Schuyler lockout ongoing, no resolution; processing-constrained dynamics continue to cap live and feeder recovery.
sections.bottom_line: Lockout still sets the ceiling; live cattle cannot rally sustainably until processing capacity comes back.
sections.vs_yesterday: Cattle: same processing-drag pattern, no new resolution signal.

sections.title: Oats Fell Hard Again
sections.body: Oats dropped **3.1%** to **$3.26**, the overnight session's only flag worth noting. This is three significant down sessions in a short window: oats fell 15% in late June, recovered briefly, ran 4.6% overnight on June 30, and are now giving most of that back. The whipsaw pattern without a clean fundamental driver says the fund exit from oats is not clean or orderly. At $3.26, oats are 47% off the 52-week low, which means there is still a cushion of long-side positioning that could unwind further. No weather or crop-specific news drove today's move. Looks like fund liquidation.
sections.catalyst: No clean news catalyst; fund positioning unwind consistent with recent volatility pattern.
sections.bottom_line: Fund exit in oats still choppy; another leg lower is more likely than a sustained base.

sections.title: Energy and Inputs: Crude Softens, Phosphate Duty Gone
sections.body: WTI crude fell 1.4% to **$68.82**, continuing a quarter-end trend that has crude down roughly 19% since early April as Iran-Hormuz tensions, with the Strait of Hormuz premium that built since early April now deflating on diplomatic progress, unwind from futures prices. An ING analyst flagged this week that oil prices may be reflecting more optimism on a U.S.-Iran peace deal than the situation has yet earned, and $68.82 supports that read: the premium is still coming out. On the inputs side, President Trump signed a temporary suspension of phosphate fertilizer duties on Moroccan imports, which had been providing about 22% estimated input cost relief for producers relying on that supply chain. Natural gas eased 0.9% to **$3.23**. Lower energy costs into the second half of the season are a quiet tailwind for operating margins, even as corn prices remain soft.
sections.catalyst: IEA flags $50B U.S. coal/gas power spend; Hormuz premium continues unwinding; Trump suspends Moroccan phosphate duties.
sections.bottom_line: Crude still deflating the Hormuz premium; phosphate duty suspension is a real input cost break.

outside_the_pit.title: Bayer Seeks Glyphosate Import Duties After Supreme Court Win
outside_the_pit.body: Days after farm groups cheered Bayer's Supreme Court glyphosate labeling victory, Monsanto filed a request with federal trade officials for import duties on foreign glyphosate. If granted, this flips a legal win into a cost hit for producers who source generic herbicide internationally. Watch for farm group pushback and a timeline on the trade ruling.

outside_the_pit.title: Australia Confirms First H5N1 Bird Flu in Wild Marine Birds
outside_the_pit.body: Western Australia detected H5N1 in wild marine birds and locked down commercial poultry farms for biosecurity checks. The U.S. commercial poultry sector has been managing its own H5N1 exposure since 2022; any spread in Australia's commercial layer flock adds to global egg supply pressure and keeps domestic biosecurity protocols in focus.

outside_the_pit.title: U.S. Rice Acres Fall to Lowest Level Since the 1970s
outside_the_pit.body: USDA's June Acreage report showed U.S. rice planted area hit a multi-decade low while cotton acres came in above expectations. University of Arkansas economist Hunter Biram says the shift reflects both market signals and input economics. For row-crop rotation managers in the Mid-South, the cotton-over-rice pivot is already reshaping what gets planted next spring.

spread_to_watch.label: December corn / nearby corn carry
spread_to_watch.commentary: December corn at $4.38 versus nearby at $4.14 is a 24-cent carry, and that spread is what the market is paying you to store corn through harvest. The carry is not widening, which tells you the funds are not adding new bear pressure on the deferred contract. If heat stress during pollination shows up in crop ratings, that December carry compresses fast as new-crop supply risk reprices upward.

basis.headline: Eastern Belt corn basis firm; western soft, consistent with seasonal.
basis.body: **Eastern Corn Belt basis is firming** as ethanol grind runs at full capacity and local demand keeps pace with supply. Producers east of the Mississippi with old-crop bushels still in storage have a window the futures board is not fully pricing. Western Belt basis staying soft, no reason to rush movement there. Cash soybean basis is quiet and not offering a premium over the board on either coast.

weekly_thread.question: Does Tuesday's USDA Acreage report confirm planted corn and soybean acres at levels that hold current futures prices, or does a surprise reprice the board?
weekly_thread.status_text: The Acreage report landed Tuesday and the stocks surprise absorbed the bearish acres verdict, keeping corn at $4.14 rather than testing lower. The question now shifts from 'did acres reprice?' to 'does the July heat wave add a premium the report couldn't?'

the_more_you_know.title: The Pollination Heat Premium Has a Short Shelf Life
the_more_you_know.body: December corn at $4.38 is sitting inside peak pollination week with 90-to-95 degree heat across most of the Belt and no stress premium in the price. That gap exists because markets price probabilities, not outcomes: the heat has to actually show up in crop condition ratings before funds buy the weather story. The window where a heat premium can be added to December corn is roughly two weeks, from now through mid-July, after which the critical pollination period closes and the weather risk window shuts. After that, the only levers left are the August WASDE production estimate and export pace. Producers watching December corn at $4.38 should understand they are watching a clock, not just a price: if this week's heat does not show up in Monday's Crop Progress ratings as a condition deterioration, the premium window closes without ever opening.

watch_list.desc: USDA Weekly Export Sales: soybean sales below 300,000 MT keeps November beans under $11.44 pressure; above 400,000 MT is the number that could bring buyers back.
USDA Crop Progress: corn good-to-excellent rating dropping below 65% is the first signal that this week's heat actually stressed the crop and warrants a December premium.
Corn Belt high temperatures: if highs stay at or above 95 degrees in Iowa, Illinois, and Indiana for three or more consecutive days during tassel, watch December corn for a move toward $4.50.
Cargill Fort Morgan/Schuyler lockout resolution: any return-to-work agreement removes the processing cap that has held live cattle near $242 for three weeks.

daily_quote.text: The investor's chief problem — and even his worst enemy — is likely to be himself.

source_summary: USDA NASS Quarterly Grain Stocks, USDA June Acreage Report, Brownfield Ag News, Farm Policy News, OilPrice.com, Compeer Financial / Dr. Megan Roberts, CME Group settlement prices.
